steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is an Inferences question. The passage from Trollope's The Warden describes Henry Vale's relationship with power and recognition. The question asks what the text most strongly suggests about him. The key details are: his glory is "hidden but still conscious," he loves to sit "silent in a corner" listening to politicians, and he privately thinks about how they are all "in his power." The answer must stay grounded in what the text directly supports.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘He flatters himself that his readers take a greater interest in him than in politicians.’: The passage says men in far-off provinces "read The Jupiter" but does not compare their interest in Vale versus politicians. Vale thinks politicians are in his power, not that readers prefer him to politicians. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘He has received much praise but tends to overestimate influential people's respect for him.’: The passage never mentions Vale receiving praise, and it says nothing about how influential people regard him. This is invented. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘He seeks mainly the admiration of readers in places that are far away.’: The passage actually says the hidden, private glory "suited the nature of the man" — he prefers unacknowledged influence. There is no indication he seeks admiration from distant readers. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘He takes private satisfaction in his capacity to influence public opinion.’: The text states that "hidden but still conscious glory suited the nature of the man." He sits silently thinking that politicians are all "in his power" — his satisfaction is private and rooted in his ability to influence through The Jupiter. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
